modules.title: Git Basics & Setup

sections.heading: What is Git
sections.content: Git is a distributed version control system. Every developer has full repo copy. GitHub hosts repos with collaboration features like PRs. Without Git you cannot work in any professional team.
sections.code:
# Configure
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@email.com"

# Init new repo
git init

# Clone existing
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

# Check status
git status
sections.tip: Run git status before and after every operation.

sections.heading: Three Areas
sections.content: Working Directory (files) → Staging Area (ready) → Repository (committed). Files flow: Working → Staging → Repo.
sections.code:
# Working → Staging
git add filename.txt
git add .                 # ALL changes

# Staging → Repository
git commit -m "Add feature"

# Shortcut tracked files
git commit -am "Fix bug"

# Unstage
git restore --staged file.txt

# Discard working changes
git restore file.txt

# See changes
git diff                  # Working vs Staging
git diff --staged         # Staging vs Last Commit
sections.tip: git add . stages everything. Make small focused commits not giant ones.

sections.heading: Viewing History
sections.content: git log shows commit history. Essential for debugging and reviews.
sections.code:
git log --oneline
git log --oneline --graph --all
git log --grep="login"
git show abc1234
git blame filename.txt
sections.tip: git log --oneline --graph --all is the most useful log command.

key_points: Git tracks locally GitHub hosts remotely
git add stages git commit saves
git status before and after everything

module_quiz.question: git add . does?
module_quiz.explanation: Moves all changes to staging area.

module_quiz.question: git commit -m saves to?
module_quiz.explanation: Saves snapshot to local repo history.

module_quiz.question: git restore file does?
options: Discards changes reverts to last commit
module_quiz.explanation: Permanently loses uncommitted changes in that file.

sections.heading: Branches
sections.content: Branches let you work on features without affecting main. Main stays stable. Feature branches hold WIP.
sections.code:
git branch                # List
git checkout -b feature   # Create + switch
git switch -c feature     # Modern way
git branch -d feature     # Delete (safe)
git branch -D feature     # Force delete

# Naming: feature/login bugfix/header hotfix/security
sections.tip: Never work directly on main. Always create feature branch.

sections.heading: Merging
sections.content: Combines changes from one branch into another. Fast-forward or three-way merge.
sections.code:
git checkout main
git merge feature-login

# --no-ff always creates merge commit
git merge --no-ff feature

# Abort merge
git merge --abort
sections.tip: Use --no-ff so git log shows the branch clearly.

sections.heading: Merge Conflicts
sections.content: Same lines changed on both branches. Git cant auto-resolve. You must manually choose.
sections.code:
<<<<<<< HEAD
const color = 'blue';     // Your version
=======
const color = 'red';      // Their version
>>>>>>> feature

# Fix: remove markers keep correct code
git add resolved-file.txt
git commit -m "Resolve conflict"
sections.tip: Dont panic. Read both versions. Remove markers. Add and commit.

module_quiz.question: git checkout -b feature?
module_quiz.explanation: Creates new branch and switches to it.

module_quiz.question: Before merge feature into main?
module_quiz.explanation: Must be ON the target branch.

module_quiz.question: Conflict markers <<< === >>>?
module_quiz.explanation: You decide which version to keep.

module_quiz.question: --no-ff flag?
module_quiz.explanation: Shows branch visually in log graph.

sections.heading: Working with Remotes
sections.content: Push sends commits up. Pull brings others down. Origin is default remote name.
sections.code:
git remote add origin https://github.com/user/repo.git
git push -u origin main
git pull origin main
git fetch origin          # Download only no merge

# Delete remote branch
git push origin --delete feature
git branch -r             # See remote branches
sections.tip: Use fetch + merge instead of pull. Fetch is safer.

sections.heading: Pull Requests
sections.content: PRs propose changes for team review before merging. Standard in all companies.
sections.code:
# 1. Create branch
git checkout -b feature/search
# 2. Work and commit
git add . && git commit -m "Add search"
# 3. Push
git push -u origin feature/search
# 4. Create PR on GitHub
# 5. Team reviews
# 6. Merge
# 7. Update local
git checkout main && git pull
sections.tip: Write clear PR descriptions. Small PRs under 300 lines get reviewed faster.

sections.heading: Forking
sections.content: Fork creates your copy of someone elses repo. For open source contributions.
sections.code:
# 1. Fork on GitHub
# 2. Clone YOUR fork
git clone https://github.com/YOU/repo.git
# 3. Add upstream
git remote add upstream https://github.com/ORIGINAL/repo.git
# 4. Branch work push
# 5. Create PR to original
sections.tip: Start with good first issue labels for open source.

module_quiz.question: git push -u origin main -u does?
options: Sets upstream for future simple push

modules.title: Undoing Changes

sections.heading: Undo Everything
sections.content: Multiple ways to undo depending on stage. Know when to use each.
sections.code:
# Undo working changes
git restore file.txt

# Undo staging
git restore --staged file.txt

# Undo last commit keep changes
git reset --soft HEAD~1

# Undo last commit DELETE changes
git reset --hard HEAD~1

# Fix last commit message
git commit --amend -m "Better msg"

# Safe undo (shared branches)
git revert abc1234

# Stash work temporarily
git stash
git stash pop              # Restore
git stash list
sections.tip: Never reset --hard on pushed commits. Use revert. Stash is your friend for branch switching.

key_points: restore for working restore --staged for staged

module_quiz.question: reset --hard HEAD~1?
options: Deletes last commit AND changes permanently
module_quiz.explanation: Cannot recover easily. Use with caution.

module_quiz.question: revert vs reset?
options: revert safe new commit reset rewrites history
module_quiz.explanation: Revert for shared. Reset for local.

module_quiz.question: commit --amend?
module_quiz.explanation: Fix message or add forgotten files.

module_quiz.question: restore --staged file?
module_quiz.explanation: Moves from staging to working. Changes preserved.

sections.heading: Git Flow vs GitHub Flow
sections.content: GitHub Flow: simple for startups. Git Flow: structured for enterprises.
sections.code:
# GITHUB FLOW:
# main always deployable
# feature branch → PR → review → merge → deploy

# GIT FLOW:
# main (production)
# develop (integration)
# feature/* → develop
# release/* → main
# hotfix/* → main
sections.tip: Use GitHub Flow for JobApp. Git Flow is overkill for small teams.

sections.heading: Good Commits
sections.content: Conventional commits help teammates and debugging.
sections.code:
# Format: type(scope): description
git commit -m "feat: add search"
git commit -m "fix: login redirect"
git commit -m "docs: update README"
git commit -m "refactor: extract utils"

# BAD: "fix stuff" "updates" "WIP"
sections.tip: Would you understand this commit 6 months later?

sections.heading: Top Git Questions
sections.content: Asked in every developer interview.
sections.code:
# merge vs rebase?
# merge: preserves branch history merge commit
# rebase: linear history rewrites commits
# NEVER rebase pushed commits

# cherry-pick?
git cherry-pick abc1234  # Apply ONE commit

# HEAD?
# Points to current commit
# HEAD~1 = one back

# fetch vs pull?
# fetch: download only (safe)
# pull: download + merge (can conflict)
sections.tip: merge vs rebase is #1 Git interview question.

sections.heading: Advanced Commands
sections.content: Senior developer daily tools.
sections.code:
# Interactive rebase
git rebase -i HEAD~3
# squash reword drop commits

# Search history
git log -S "searchText"

# Compare branches
git diff main..feature

# Clean untracked
git clean -fd

# Aliases
git config --global alias.co checkout
git config --global alias.lg "log --oneline --graph --all"
sections.tip: Rebase -i before PR cleans messy commits.

module_quiz.question: cherry-pick?
options: Apply ONE commit to current branch

module_quiz.question: HEAD?
module_quiz.explanation: Pointer that moves with checkout and commit.

sections.heading: CI/CD
sections.content: Automatically run tests and deploy on push.
sections.code:
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
    - run: npm install
    - run: npm test
    - run: npm run build
sections.tip: Set up CI Day 1.

sections.heading: Releases
sections.content: Tags and semantic versioning for releases.
sections.code:
git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "First release"
git push origin v1.0.0

# MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
# 1.0.0 → 1.0.1 patch
# 1.0.0 → 1.1.0 minor
# 1.0.0 → 2.0.0 major

# Link PR to issue
git commit -m "fix: crash (fixes #42)"
sections.tip: Semantic versioning is industry standard.

module_quiz.question: Actions does?
options: Auto tests and deploys on push

module_quiz.question: Fixes #42?
module_quiz.explanation: GitHub closes issue when PR merges.

sections.heading: Code Review
sections.content: Reviews catch bugs and share knowledge.
sections.code:
# Reviewer checklist:
# Bugs edge cases
# Duplication
# Naming clarity
# Missing tests
# Security

# Author rules:
# PRs under 300 lines
# Clear description
# Self-review first
# Dont take feedback personally

# Branch protection:
# Require reviews
# Require CI pass
# No direct push to main
sections.tip: Small PRs under 300 lines get reviewed in hours.

sections.heading: Team Workflow
sections.content: Daily professional Git routine.
sections.code:
# Morning:
git checkout main && git pull
git checkout feature && git rebase main

# Before PR:
git rebase -i HEAD~5    # Clean commits
git push --force-with-lease

# After merge:
git checkout main && git pull
git branch -d feature
sections.tip: --force-with-lease safer than --force.

sections.heading: Hooks and Bisect
sections.content: Hooks run scripts on events. Bisect finds bugs.
sections.code:
# Pre-commit hook
#!/bin/sh
npm run lint || exit 1

# Husky (popular tool)
npm install husky --save-dev

# Bisect — find bug commit
git bisect start
git bisect bad
git bisect good v1.0
# Binary search until found
git bisect reset

# Reflog — recover anything
git reflog
git checkout abc1234

# Worktrees
git worktree add ../hotfix main
sections.tip: git reflog is your emergency recovery tool.

sections.heading: Security
sections.content: Never commit secrets. Use SSH. Sign commits.
sections.code:
# .gitignore essentials
node_modules/
.env
.env.local
*.key
dist/
build/

# If pushed secret:
# 1. REVOKE KEY IMMEDIATELY
# 2. Clean history

# SSH setup
ssh-keygen -t ed25519
# Add to GitHub Settings
sections.tip: Pushed API key? CHANGE IT. Its in history forever.

sections.heading: Productivity
sections.content: Aliases and speed tips.
sections.code:
git config --global alias.co checkout
git config --global alias.br branch
git config --global alias.st status
git config --global alias.cm "commit -m"
git config --global alias.lg "log --oneline --graph --all"

# Tips:
# Tab complete branch names
# Stash frequently
# Commit often push less
# .gitignore first file
sections.tip: Aliases save thousands of keystrokes per year.

module_quiz.question: Pushed API key first step?
module_quiz.explanation: Key in history. Must revoke first.
